result:
The Moon is drifting away from us at the width of a crayon every year—about 3.8 centimeters—yet we still talk about “lunar permanence” as if tomorrow night it won’t be a sliver farther off, a sliver more aloof. I keep a battered blue notebook in which I log distances: Moon-to-Earth, 2023; my childhood home-to-here; the number of silent weeks since I last answered my father’s texts. The columns look like tide tables for places I’ll never visit. They comfort me in the same way bus schedules comfort insomniacs at 3 a.m.—proof that something, somewhere, is planning to depart on time even if we’re not.

Yesterday I watched a man on the subway fold an origami swan from a yellowed parking ticket. He worked slowly, licking his thumb to crease the paper, while the train lurched through its fossilized tunnels. When he finished, he tucked the swan behind the plastic barrier that says “Do Not Lean.” By the time we reached my stop, three strangers had photographed it. A fourth had written something on its wing—maybe a phone number, maybe a tiny confession. The swan will ride the rails all day, gathering fingerprints and secondhand dreams, becoming a pocket-sized palimpsest of anonymous longing. I keep thinking about how paper, once creased, retains a memory of the fold forever. You can flatten it, frame it, burn it; the ghost of the bend remains, a scar of intention.

Sometimes I wonder if the same is true of people. We crease each other in passing: a shared joke in a grocery line, the accidental brush of fingers when handing back a dropped glove. Those micro-creases don’t show up on skin, but they settle somewhere deeper, in the fiber of who we believe ourselves to be. I can still feel the grocery-store cashier who, twelve years ago, looked at my haul of ramen and off-brand tea and said, “College vet?” When I nodded, she slipped a free chocolate bar into the bag and whispered, “You’ll survive.” I never saw her again. I don’t even remember her face. But that single kindness—crease—keeps unfolding inside me whenever I stock up on comfort food at 2 a.m., a private origami crane of morale.

Last night the city smelled like hot metal and lilacs, the contradictory signature of late spring. I walked until the sodium streetlights thinned and the sky remembered it was black. Somewhere overhead, the International Space Station was scheduled to pass, a bright apostrophe against the dark. I waited, neck craned, feeling the smallness that horizon-gazers crave. It never showed—clouds, haze, orbital whim. Standing there, I realized anticipation itself can be the event: the hush, the tilted head, the calibrated hope. We are always waiting for something—transit, test results, love, dawn. The trick is recognizing that the interval isn’t emptiness; it’s origami paper before the first fold, infinite potential disguised as a blank rectangle.

I keep thinking I should mail my father a letter, something tactile that smells like coffee and candle smoke. I’d tell him the Moon is leaving us but we still have tides, so farewells can be gradual and survivable. I’d enclose a flattened swan, though I’ve never mastered origami. I’d apologize for the creases I never made, the conversations we left unfolded. Maybe I’d just send him this page, torn from the blue notebook, edges serrated like a row of tiny teeth. He could hold it up to the light and read the indentation of words I never wrote: I’m still on the train, still folding paper, still measuring distances that shrink the moment I look away.

Tomorrow I’ll wake before the Sun, when the city is just a charcoal rumor. I’ll walk to the river where the barges hum dirges in diesel minor. I’ll throw a blank sheet into the current and pretend it’s a message, a map, a moon. The water will fold it once, twice, until it disappears. Somewhere downstream, someone will find a soggy wedge of paper, smooth it against a thigh, and see only ruin. But the river will know better: every crease is a stanza, every tear a syllable in the long, illegible poem we keep writing together, one crayon-width of departure at a time.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
